title: Wet-felted bath mat
subtitle: A 60 × 40 cm dense merino mat, fully fulled to handle daily foot traffic
excerpt: A large-scale wet-felted bath mat in 100% merino roving. Six to eight cross-hatch layers totalling 400-500 g of roving produce a mat at least 12 mm thick after fulling. Requires 45-60 minutes of sustained rolling. An optional cut-rubber anti-slip backing is added after drying.
sourceNotes: Wet-felted bath mats are a large-scale project found in intermediate UK wet-felting workshops and online fibre arts communities. Technique synthesised from general wet-felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for wet felting or spinning.
The final agitation stage of wet felting in which fibres are compressed and shrunk by rubbing, rolling, and throwing, producing a dense, firm fabric.
A laying technique in which successive tufts of roving are placed at 90 degrees to the previous layer, producing even felting in all directions.
Wool fibres that have begun to bond but have not yet fully felted. The surface holds together but the piece can still be shaped.
A layer of rubber or latex matting attached to the underside of a rug or mat to prevent it moving on smooth bathroom floors.

content.text: A wet-felted bath mat is one of the most demanding wet felting projects in terms of physical effort and scale. At 60 × 40 cm finished size, it requires 400-500 g of merino
roving
laid in six to eight
cross-hatch layers
and sustained
fulling

for 45-60 minutes in total. The intermediate rating reflects the stamina and scale required rather than complex technique: the method is the same flat wet felting used for a pot holder, scaled up considerably. The finished mat needs to be at least 12 mm thick to hold up to daily foot traffic. An optional cut-rubber
anti-slip backing
is attached after drying to prevent the mat moving on wet bathroom tiles. The
pre-felt
dimensions are approximately 90 × 60 cm, allowing for approximately 33% shrinkage in each direction.

items.name: Merino roving, 19-21 micron, any colour or combination
Felting net (polyester mesh), at least 100 × 70 cm
Bamboo rolling mat, at least 100 cm long
Craft scissors (for trimming edge and cutting rubber backing)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the working area

This project works best on a large table or on the floor on a waterproof sheet. The pre-felt dimensions (90 × 60 cm) are too large for most kitchen tables. A trestle table or cleared dining table works well. Lay the bamboo rolling mat on the working surface, then place the felting net on top. Have a large bowl or bucket of warm soapy water ready nearby.
Step 2: Lay the roving in cross-hatch layers

Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them in a rectangle 90 × 60 cm. The first layer runs horizontally (along the 90 cm dimension). The second layer runs vertically. Continue alternating until all eight layers are laid. Each layer should be thin and even: thick patches will create lumps in the finished mat. Divide the total roving weight equally across all eight layers before starting, so you do not run short at layer six or seven.
Step 3: Wet down through the net

Place the felting net over the full surface. Pour warm soapy water (40°C) slowly and evenly over the net, working from the centre outward. Pour in sections: pour, then press firmly through the net with flat hands, then pour again. The full stack needs to be saturated throughout, not just on the surface. This takes more water than seems necessary: at least 4 litres.
Step 4: Rub through the net

Still working through the net, rub the surface with flat hands using firm circular movements. Cover the full 90 × 60 cm area systematically: work in rows from one short edge to the other. Rub for 15 minutes without stopping. The roving layers should begin to bond and the surface should feel increasingly firm. Lift a corner of the net every few minutes to check that the surface is holding together. Add more soapy water if the surface starts to feel tacky or dry.
Step 5: Roll in the bamboo mat, stage one

Remove the felting net. Carefully roll the pre-felt panel inside the bamboo mat lengthwise (rolling along the 90 cm dimension). This requires a bamboo mat of at least 100 cm: if your mat is shorter, work in two half-lengths. Roll the mat back and forth on the table with firm pressure for 10 minutes. Unroll and check the surface: the fibres should be noticeably more bonded. Turn the panel 90 degrees and roll again for 10 minutes.
Step 6: Roll in the bamboo mat, stage two

Re-wet the panel with fresh hot soapy water (as hot as your hands can tolerate: up to 50°C). Roll again in all four orientations, 10 minutes each direction. This is the most physically demanding stage. Take breaks as needed. The mat will have shrunk noticeably from the 90 × 60 cm pre-felt dimensions.
Step 7: Full to finished dimensions

Continue fulling: throw the mat onto the felting mat 30 times, fold in thirds and throw again 30 times. Scrunch and squeeze the full mat in both hands, working systematically from edge to centre. Measure after every 10 minutes: the intended finished size is 60 × 40 cm. Check the thickness: compress the mat with your palm. If it compresses more than 2 mm under firm hand pressure, continue fulling. The mat needs to be at least 12 mm thick. Total fulling time from Step 5 onward: 45-60 minutes.
Step 8: Rinse, press, and dry

Rinse the mat thoroughly in water the same temperature as the final wash water (to avoid thermal shock). The rinse water will run soapy for several rounds: rinse until the water is clear. Press out excess water by rolling the mat in large towels and treading on the bundle. Lay the mat flat to dry. Pulling the edges out to a rectangular shape while damp helps the mat dry evenly. Do not dry near direct heat. Full drying may take 24-48 hours given the mat's thickness.
Step 9: Add the anti-slip backing (optional)

Once fully dry, cut a piece of rubber anti-slip matting to 59 × 39 cm (1 cm smaller than the mat on each edge so it does not show at the perimeter). Lay the rubber on the underside of the felt mat. It can be left loose (the weight of the felt holds it in position), or secured with a few stitches of strong thread through the rubber and felt at the corners.

items.problem: The mat is unevenly thick: thin in the centre, thicker at the edges
items.cause: Roving was distributed unevenly during laying, with more material at the edges than the centre
items.fix: When laying, weigh each roving layer individually and ensure even distribution. Pay particular attention to the centre, which is often neglected in favour of building up edges.

items.problem: The mat has finished at less than 12 mm thick
items.cause: Insufficient roving used, or fulling was stopped too early
items.fix: For next time, use 500 g of roving rather than 400 g. If the current mat is too thin, it can be used as a bathside mat rather than a full bath mat.

items.problem: The finished mat has a wavy, curled edge
items.cause: Roving at the edge was thinner than the body, causing differential shrinkage
items.fix: While the mat is still wet after rinsing, stretch and pin the edges flat on a foam mat and let it dry pinned. Once dry, the edges will hold flat.

items.problem: The mat is pilling heavily underfoot after a few uses
items.cause: The surface was not fully felted; loose fibres remain at the surface
items.fix: Wet the mat with warm soapy water and scrub the surface hard with a stiff-bristled brush for 10 minutes, then rinse and dry. This will full the surface fibres into the body.

content.text: Care and finishing

Machine-wash on a wool cycle at 30°C without spin, or hand-wash in cool water. Press flat with hands while wet, reshape if needed, and lay flat to dry. Do not tumble dry: the mat will felt further and shrink. Remove the rubber backing before washing and reposition after the mat is dry. With regular use, the surface may develop a slight nap which can be smoothed with a damp cloth.
